column1: 27-year-old man after blunt thoracic trauma R8K2Q9M4P6TZ
column3:
AP chest radiograph of a 27-year-old man involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision shows rightward shift of the upper trachea , a contour abnormality and widening of the upper mediastinum , a large left hemothorax and associated left apical cap, wide left intercostal spaces, and rightward mediastinal shift.
Coronal CECT of the same patient shows the underlying traumatic aortic injury characterized by an intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and a large contained rupture .
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury

Keyword findings:
rightward shift of the upper trachea
contour abnormality and widening of the upper mediastinum
large left hemothorax and associated left apical cap
intimomedial flap
contour irregularity
large contained rupture

Tight reasoning:
The AP radiograph supplies the indirect trauma clues: mediastinal widening, aortic contour abnormality, tracheal shift, hemothorax, and left apical cap. The CECT then shows the direct aortic wall findings emphasized by RadPrimer/Core: intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and contained rupture. Together these establish acute traumatic aortic injury rather than a nonspecific wide mediastinum.

Differential:
Wide mediastinum of other etiology
Ductus diverticulum
Atherosclerotic ulceration

column21:
🩻 Traumatic Aortic Injury
Traumatic aortic injury is an aortic wall disruption, usually from high-velocity blunt deceleration trauma, with CTA as the key diagnostic and treatment-planning test. The dominant board move is to connect indirect radiographic mediastinal hemorrhage signs to direct CTA signs, then separate true injury from isthmus mimics.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer synthesis; Core coverage from Core Radiology, 2nd ed., Vascular Imaging, Aortic trauma, Vascular:547-548, plus IR:604 treatment note; RadPrimer article: Traumatic Aortic Injury.
🧾 Abbreviations / Synonyms
  • TAI = traumatic aortic injury; related terms include ATAI, BTAI, BAT, BAI, BTAR, aortic transection, and traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
🧭 Overview / Mechanism
  • Aortic wall disruption ranges from intimomedial injury to transection; blunt rapid deceleration is most common, while penetrating trauma is less common.
  • Core emphasizes relatively fixed injury levels: aortic root, isthmus, and hiatus; the aortic isthmus is the most common site.
  • RadPrimer localizes the isthmus injury to the distal aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum and states the aortic isthmus accounts for about 90% of cases.
  • Mechanisms include rapid differential deceleration/shear, torsion/stretching, water-hammer intraaortic pressure, viscous response, and osseous pinch.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • Radiography / initial trauma clue
    • Wide mediastinum and aortic contour abnormality are the best initial radiographic clues.
    • Normal chest radiograph is exceedingly rare; RadPrimer gives a negative predictive value of 98%.
    • Indirect signs include mediastinal widening, AP window obscuration, tracheal/tube deviation, left apical cap, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripe, and inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus.
    • A first rib fracture indicates severe trauma and possible TAI.
  • CTA
    • CTA is the imaging modality of choice / Core gold standard for suspected traumatic aortic injury.
    • Direct signs: intimomedial flap or intimal irregularity, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, irregular aortic contour, abrupt caliber change, dissection flap, intramural hematoma, and rarely active extravasation.
    • Indirect sign: periaortic or mediastinal hemorrhage; hemorrhage contacting the aortic wall is concerning, while hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by fat plane may be venous.
    • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% negative predictive value for excluding TAI.
  • Other modalities
    • MR generally has no role in acute trauma; it may be used to identify or follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
    • TEE can demonstrate intimal tear, transection, and hemopericardium; it is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
    • Angiography was the prior gold standard and is now used for endovascular repair.
🎯 Classification / Management Pivots
  • Society of Vascular Surgery grading: Grade I intimal injury/minimal aortic injury; Grade II intramural hematoma; Grade III pseudoaneurysm; Grade IV rupture.
  • Minimal aortic injury: subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality without external contour deformity; Core notes intimal-only lesions with small flap less than 1 cm and no/little periaortic hematoma.
  • Grade I is the only injury routinely managed nonoperatively with close monitoring and CTA follow-up; RadPrimer notes growing support for selected grade II/III conservative management.
  • Anti-impulse / blood pressure control is paramount and reduces rupture risk from 12% to 1.5%; RadPrimer goal: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.
  • TEVAR is recommended over open repair for grade II-IV injuries when anatomy is suitable; open repair is reserved for anatomy precluding TEVAR.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis / Mimics
  • Wide mediastinum false positives include rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, and tortuous great vessels.
  • Ductus diverticulum is an anteromedial isthmus outpouching with smooth, gently sloping shoulders and no intimomedial flap.
  • Other mimics include fusiform proximal descending aortic enlargement, aortic spindle, atherosclerotic ulceration, bronchial-intercostal trunk infundibulum, artifacts, plaque, and adjacent opacified vessels.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Contained rupture or pseudoaneurysm should not be reported in a way that implies stability.
  • Chronic pseudoaneurysm occurs in about 2% of survivors and may appear as a peripherally calcified mass at the aortopulmonary window with thrombus.
  • Thin-section CTA with multiplanar reformations is essential for differentiating true injury from mimics; high-pitch or cardiac-gated CT may help with equivocal pulsation or motion artifacts.
⭐ Super Summary
  • In blunt high-energy trauma, do not stop at a wide mediastinum: CTA looks for a direct aortic wall sign, especially at the isthmus.
  • The safest discriminator is wall injury plus adjacent periaortic hematoma; smooth isthmus outpouching without flap or hemorrhage favors ductus diverticulum.
  • Management hinges on grade and stability: minimal intimal injuries can be observed; pseudoaneurysm/rupture-level injuries move toward TEVAR and anti-impulse control.

column1: 12-year-old boy after blunt thoracic trauma N5Q8L2T7X4PR
column3:
AP chest radiograph of a 12-year-old boy involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision shows a wide mediastinum, mild rightward displacement of endotracheal and gastric tubes secondary to aortic injury, and mediastinal hemorrhage. Note concurrent right pleural effusion and bilateral pulmonary contusions .
Sagittal CECT of the same patient shows the classic location of aortic injury at the isthmus with focal aortic disruption and pseudoaneurysm formation.
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury

Keyword findings:
wide mediastinum
rightward displacement of endotracheal and gastric tubes
mediastinal hemorrhage
right pleural effusion
bilateral pulmonary contusions
classic location at the isthmus
focal aortic disruption
pseudoaneurysm formation

Tight reasoning:
The chest radiograph shows mediastinal hemorrhage physiology with tube displacement and a wide mediastinum in severe blunt trauma. The sagittal CECT localizes the direct injury to the aortic isthmus, the classic and most common site in RadPrimer/Core, with focal disruption and pseudoaneurysm. That combination makes acute traumatic aortic injury the single best diagnosis.

Differential:
Wide mediastinum of other etiology
Ductus diverticulum
Aortic spindle

column1: 43-year-old woman after blunt thoracic trauma H7M3V9K2L5QA
column3:
Axial CECT of a 43-year-old woman involved in a motor vehicle collision shows an intimomedial flap at the aortic isthmus, associated intramural hematoma , and concurrent left hemothorax.
Parasagittal CECT of the same patient shows the location of the aortic injury at the aortic isthmus with resultant focal outpouching , consistent with pseudoaneurysm formation, which results as the aortic adventitia "contains" the focal aortic disruption, but should not imply stability.
column5:
Traumatic aortic injury with pseudoaneurysm

Keyword findings:
intimomedial flap at the aortic isthmus
associated intramural hematoma
concurrent left hemothorax
location at the aortic isthmus
resultant focal outpouching
pseudoaneurysm formation
adventitia "contains" the focal aortic disruption

Tight reasoning:
The axial image provides direct signs of aortic wall injury: intimomedial flap and intramural hematoma at the isthmus. The parasagittal image shows the downstream morphology, a focal outpouching/pseudoaneurysm produced when the adventitia contains the disruption. The caption explicitly warns that this containment should not imply stability.

Differential:
Ductus diverticulum
Infundibulum of bronchial-intercostal trunk
Atherosclerotic ulceration

column1: 65-year-old man with no acute symptoms J4X8N2Q6M7TC
column3:
Lateral chest radiograph of an asymptomatic 65-year-old man shows a rounded peripherally calcified opacity in the middle mediastinum.
Axial CECT of the same patient shows a focal pseudoaneurysm at the level of the aortic isthmus with peripheral calcification and eccentric intraluminal thrombus, corresponding to the radiographic abnormality. Upon further inquiry, the patient reported a history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously.
column5:
Chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm

Keyword findings:
rounded peripherally calcified opacity
middle mediastinum
focal pseudoaneurysm at the level of the aortic isthmus
peripheral calcification
eccentric intraluminal thrombus
history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously

Tight reasoning:
The radiograph shows the chronic radiographic clue: a rounded, peripherally calcified middle mediastinal/AP-window opacity. CECT confirms a calcified isthmus pseudoaneurysm with eccentric thrombus and remote high-speed trauma history. This matches RadPrimer/Core teaching that chronic pseudoaneurysm may develop after missed or untreated aortic injury and often has peripheral calcification and thrombus.

Differential:
Thoracic aortic aneurysm
Calcified mediastinal lymph node
Aorticopulmonary window mass

column1: 30-year-old man after blunt chest trauma Q2L9K6T4M8ZA
column3: Sagittal CECT of a 30-year-old man who sustained blunt chest trauma shows a contour irregularity and a focal aortic pseudoaneurysm . Adjacent mediastinal hemorrhage helps distinguish injury from branch vessel infundibulum.
column5:
Traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm

Keyword findings:
blunt chest trauma
contour irregularity
focal aortic pseudoaneurysm
adjacent mediastinal hemorrhage
distinguish injury from branch vessel infundibulum

Tight reasoning:
The key is not just a focal contour bump; it is a focal aortic pseudoaneurysm with contour irregularity and adjacent mediastinal hemorrhage. RadPrimer/Core emphasize that direct aortic wall abnormality plus hemorrhage in contact with the aortic wall supports true traumatic aortic injury rather than a branch vessel infundibulum or other smooth mimic.

Differential:
Infundibulum of bronchial-intercostal trunk
Ductus diverticulum
Atherosclerotic ulceration

column1: 27-year-old man after blunt thoracic trauma R8K2Q9M4P6TZ [DD1]
column3:
AP chest radiograph of a 27-year-old man involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision shows rightward shift of the upper trachea , a contour abnormality and widening of the upper mediastinum , a large left hemothorax and associated left apical cap, wide left intercostal spaces, and rightward mediastinal shift.
Coronal CECT of the same patient shows the underlying traumatic aortic injury characterized by an intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, and a large contained rupture .
column7: Chest radiograph and CT. Based on the images, name 4 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Traumatic aortic injury
Wide mediastinum of other etiology
Ductus diverticulum
Atherosclerotic ulceration
column9:
Traumatic aortic injury: direct aortic wall abnormality on CTA such as intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, or intramural hematoma, especially with periaortic/mediastinal hemorrhage.
Wide mediastinum of other etiology: radiographic widening can be false-positive from rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, or tortuous great vessels; CTA lacks direct wall injury.
Ductus diverticulum: smooth, gently sloping isthmus outpouching with no intimomedial flap and no adjacent hemorrhage.
Atherosclerotic ulceration: irregular or ulcerated plaque, more common in older patients; use plaque morphology and absence of acute traumatic hematoma to separate from TAI.

column1: 65-year-old man with no acute symptoms J4X8N2Q6M7TC [DD2]
column3:
Lateral chest radiograph of an asymptomatic 65-year-old man shows a rounded peripherally calcified opacity in the middle mediastinum.
Axial CECT of the same patient shows a focal pseudoaneurysm at the level of the aortic isthmus with peripheral calcification and eccentric intraluminal thrombus, corresponding to the radiographic abnormality. Upon further inquiry, the patient reported a history of trauma due to a high-speed motor vehicle collision 25 years previously.
column7: Chest radiograph and CT. Based on the images, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm
Thoracic aortic aneurysm
Aorticopulmonary window mass/adenopathy
column9:
Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: the core problem is a chronic peripherally calcified AP-window/middle mediastinal abnormality, not an acute mediastinal hemorrhage pattern.
Chronic post-traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm: focal pseudoaneurysm at the aortic isthmus with peripheral calcification and eccentric thrombus, especially with remote high-speed trauma history.
Thoracic aortic aneurysm: broader aneurysmal enlargement of the aorta rather than a focal post-traumatic isthmus pseudoaneurysm.
Aorticopulmonary window mass/adenopathy: abnormal AP-window convexity may reflect lymph nodes/mass, but CT does not show direct continuity with a focal aortic pseudoaneurysm.

column15: Define traumatic aortic injury.

column15: Define minimal aortic injury.
column16:
Source definition: Subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality with no external contour deformity.
Refined operational definition based on the allowed source set: Intimal-only traumatic aortic injury, often a small intimal flap less than 1 cm with no or little periaortic hematoma.
Boundary condition: This corresponds to Grade I/intimal injury and is not a pseudoaneurysm or rupture.

column15: Define traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
column16:
Source definition: Saccular outpouching / contained rupture held only by adventitia or surrounding connective tissues.
Boundary condition: It is not a stable aneurysm; contained rupture should not imply stability.
Synonyms: post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm, contained rupture.

column11: Why is the aortic isthmus the classic location for traumatic aortic injury?
column12: The injury mechanism begins with rapid deceleration. Mobile and relatively fixed aortic segments move differently, concentrating shear, torsion, and stretching at fixation points. The isthmus is tethered near the ligamentum arteriosum, so a rapid change in velocity concentrates wall stress there. RadPrimer/Core also support other contributing forces including water-hammer intraaortic pressure and osseous pinch against the thoracic spine. Imaging consequence: the most important CTA search zone is the isthmus, where direct signs such as intimomedial flap, contour irregularity, pseudoaneurysm, or intramural hematoma are most expected.

column11: Why does the relationship of mediastinal hemorrhage to the aortic wall matter on trauma CTA?
column12: Mediastinal hemorrhage in trauma is often venous, but the interpretation pivots on whether the hemorrhage contacts the aortic wall. Core states that mediastinal hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by a fat plane can be presumed venous and treated conservatively, whereas hemorrhage in contact with the aortic wall is suggestive of aortic injury and necessitates surgical treatment. Imaging consequence: do not diagnose TAI from mediastinal blood alone; look for direct wall injury and whether a preserved fat plane separates the hematoma from the aorta.

column11: Why should a traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm or contained rupture not be described as stable?
column12: A pseudoaneurysm forms when the aortic wall disruption extends beyond the intima/media and is contained only by adventitia or surrounding mediastinal connective tissues. RadPrimer states that a Grade III pseudoaneurysm is contained by adventitia and that Grade IV rupture/adventitial injury can rapidly exsanguinate; the selected caption also states that adventitial containment should not imply stability. Imaging consequence: a focal outpouching/contained rupture is a high-risk wall disruption, not a reassuring chronic aneurysm label, and reporting should avoid language that downplays rupture risk.
column21:
🩻 Traumatic Aortic Injury
Traumatic aortic injury is an aortic wall disruption, usually from high-velocity blunt deceleration trauma, with CTA as the key diagnostic and treatment-planning test. The dominant board move is to connect indirect radiographic mediastinal hemorrhage signs to direct CTA signs, then separate true injury from isthmus mimics.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer synthesis; Core coverage from Core Radiology, 2nd ed., Vascular Imaging, Aortic trauma, Vascular:547-548, plus IR:604 treatment note; RadPrimer article: Traumatic Aortic Injury.
🧾 Abbreviations / Synonyms
  • TAI = traumatic aortic injury; related terms include ATAI, BTAI, BAT, BAI, BTAR, aortic transection, and traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
🧭 Overview / Mechanism
  • Aortic wall disruption ranges from intimomedial injury to transection; blunt rapid deceleration is most common, while penetrating trauma is less common.
  • Core emphasizes relatively fixed injury levels: aortic root, isthmus, and hiatus; the aortic isthmus is the most common site.
  • RadPrimer localizes the isthmus injury to the distal aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum and states the aortic isthmus accounts for about 90% of cases.
  • Mechanisms include rapid differential deceleration/shear, torsion/stretching, water-hammer intraaortic pressure, viscous response, and osseous pinch.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • Radiography / initial trauma clue
    • Wide mediastinum and aortic contour abnormality are the best initial radiographic clues.
    • Normal chest radiograph is exceedingly rare; RadPrimer gives a negative predictive value of 98%.
    • Indirect signs include mediastinal widening, AP window obscuration, tracheal/tube deviation, left apical cap, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripe, and inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus.
    • A first rib fracture indicates severe trauma and possible TAI.
  • CTA
    • CTA is the imaging modality of choice / Core gold standard for suspected traumatic aortic injury.
    • Direct signs: intimomedial flap or intimal irregularity, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, irregular aortic contour, abrupt caliber change, dissection flap, intramural hematoma, and rarely active extravasation.
    • Indirect sign: periaortic or mediastinal hemorrhage; hemorrhage contacting the aortic wall is concerning, while hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by fat plane may be venous.
    • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% negative predictive value for excluding TAI.
  • Other modalities
    • MR generally has no role in acute trauma; it may be used to identify or follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
    • TEE can demonstrate intimal tear, transection, and hemopericardium; it is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
    • Angiography was the prior gold standard and is now used for endovascular repair.
🎯 Classification / Management Pivots
  • Society of Vascular Surgery grading: Grade I intimal injury/minimal aortic injury; Grade II intramural hematoma; Grade III pseudoaneurysm; Grade IV rupture.
  • Minimal aortic injury: subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality without external contour deformity; Core notes intimal-only lesions with small flap less than 1 cm and no/little periaortic hematoma.
  • Grade I is the only injury routinely managed nonoperatively with close monitoring and CTA follow-up; RadPrimer notes growing support for selected grade II/III conservative management.
  • Anti-impulse / blood pressure control is paramount and reduces rupture risk from 12% to 1.5%; RadPrimer goal: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.
  • TEVAR is recommended over open repair for grade II-IV injuries when anatomy is suitable; open repair is reserved for anatomy precluding TEVAR.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis / Mimics
  • Wide mediastinum false positives include rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, and tortuous great vessels.
  • Ductus diverticulum is an anteromedial isthmus outpouching with smooth, gently sloping shoulders and no intimomedial flap.
  • Other mimics include fusiform proximal descending aortic enlargement, aortic spindle, atherosclerotic ulceration, bronchial-intercostal trunk infundibulum, artifacts, plaque, and adjacent opacified vessels.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Contained rupture or pseudoaneurysm should not be reported in a way that implies stability.
  • Chronic pseudoaneurysm occurs in about 2% of survivors and may appear as a peripherally calcified mass at the aortopulmonary window with thrombus.
  • Thin-section CTA with multiplanar reformations is essential for differentiating true injury from mimics; high-pitch or cardiac-gated CT may help with equivocal pulsation or motion artifacts.
⭐ Super Summary
  • In blunt high-energy trauma, do not stop at a wide mediastinum: CTA looks for a direct aortic wall sign, especially at the isthmus.
  • The safest discriminator is wall injury plus adjacent periaortic hematoma; smooth isthmus outpouching without flap or hemorrhage favors ductus diverticulum.
  • Management hinges on grade and stability: minimal intimal injuries can be observed; pseudoaneurysm/rupture-level injuries move toward TEVAR and anti-impulse control.

column13: What reporting trap occurs with the phrase "contained rupture," and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Treating the word "contained" as if it means stable or low risk.
Safety check: Recognize that the pseudoaneurysm is contained only by adventitia or adjacent mediastinal structures.
Key discriminator logic: A pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture is a direct sign of aortic wall disruption and corresponds to a higher-grade injury than minimal intimal injury.
Why the error occurs: The wording sounds reassuring, but the source explicitly warns that pseudoaneurysm or "contained rupture" should not imply stability.

column1: 58-year-old man after blunt chest trauma V6P2N8Q4L9KT [BT1]
column3: Sagittal CECT of a 58-year-old man who sustained blunt chest trauma shows a smooth caliber change of the aortic isthmus adjacent to a calcified ligamentum arteriosum . Absence of adjacent hemorrhage should prompt consideration of ductus diverticulum.
column10: Sagittal CECT of a 58-year-old man who sustained blunt chest trauma shows a smooth caliber change of the aortic isthmus adjacent to a calcified ligamentum arteriosum . Absence of adjacent hemorrhage should prompt consideration of ductus diverticulum.
column13: What is the pitfall in this trauma CT case, and what safety check prevents overcalling the emergent diagnosis?
column14:
Trap: Overcalling a smooth aortic isthmus contour variant as traumatic aortic injury because the clinical setting is blunt trauma.
Safety check: Look for smooth gently sloping morphology, absence of intimomedial flap, and absence of adjacent mediastinal/periaortic hemorrhage.
Key discriminator logic: True traumatic pseudoaneurysm is typically irregular or accompanied by direct wall injury/adjacent hemorrhage; ductus diverticulum is smooth and lacks hemorrhage.
Why the error occurs: The isthmus is the classic TAI site, so anchoring bias can make a normal ductus diverticulum look suspicious.

column15: How much does adequate blood pressure control reduce rupture risk in traumatic aortic injury?
column16:
Rupture risk reduction: 12% -> 1.5%.
Exam pivot: Anti-impulse control is not optional supportive care; it is a rupture-risk intervention.

column15: What is the source-stated healing behavior of minimal aortic injury?
column16:
85-90% of minimal aortic injuries heal spontaneously within 4-8 weeks.
Exam pivot: This supports close nonoperative monitoring for Grade I injury, not complacency for higher-grade injury.

column15: What treatment is recommended for grade II-IV traumatic aortic injuries when anatomy is suitable?
column16:
TEVAR is recommended for grade II-IV traumatic aortic injuries when anatomy is suitable.
It is recommended over open repair for all age groups with suitable anatomy and is feasible in many polytrauma patients.
Source nuance: urgent repair within 24 hours is recommended, although emerging data suggest delayed repair after patient optimization may decrease mortality in selected patients.
Exam pivot: Grade II-IV injury is not the routine observation category; suitable anatomy moves the patient toward endovascular repair.

column15: What mortality/time outcome is stated for untreated traumatic aortic injury?
column16:
50% expire within 24 hours if untreated.
Exam pivot: This is why the diagnosis is urgent even when initial signs/symptoms are nonspecific.

column15: What is the key CT discriminator between ductus diverticulum and traumatic aortic injury?
column16:
Ductus diverticulum: smooth anteromedial isthmus outpouching with gently sloping shoulders, no intimomedial flap, and no adjacent hemorrhage.
TAI: direct wall injury such as flap, irregular contour, pseudoaneurysm, or hematoma in contact with the aortic wall.
Exam pivot: Smooth morphology plus no hemorrhage prevents overcalling the isthmus mimic.

column15: What CTA mimics of traumatic aortic injury are explicitly listed by RadPrimer?
column16:
Mimics include:
Pulsation/streak/motion artifacts.
Atherosclerotic plaque.
Ductus diverticulum.
Opacified adjacent vessels: bronchial artery, left superior intercostal vein, hemiazygos vein.
Exam pivot: Thin-section multiplanar reformations and gating/high-pitch acquisition help resolve equivocal mimic cases.

column15: What is the role of MR in traumatic aortic injury evaluation?
column16:
MR generally has no role in acute trauma evaluation because critically injured patients are usually unsuitable for MR.
Source-supported exception: MR may be used to identify or follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
Exam pivot: CTA remains the diagnostic center; MR is not the acute triage tool.

column15: What is the source-stated distribution of traumatic aortic injury by aortic level?
column16:
Aortic isthmus: 90%, classically at the distal arch near the ligamentum arteriosum and often on the medial aspect.
Aortic root: 5-14%, with rare survival.
Diaphragmatic hiatus: 1-12%, sometimes associated with diaphragmatic injury.
Multifocal aortic injury is uncommon.
Source basis: RadPrimer provides the percentages; Core supports fixed injury levels and the isthmus as the most common site.

column15: On trauma chest radiography, what mediastinal-widening threshold should trigger concern for traumatic aortic injury?
column16:
Mediastinal widening is source-stated as >8 cm or >25% of the transthoracic diameter.
It is an indirect sign of mediastinal hemorrhage, seen in 30-70% of patients: sensitive but not specific.
Exam pivot: radiographic mediastinal widening should trigger CTA/further investigation, but the diagnosis requires direct aortic evaluation.

column15: When is open surgical repair used for traumatic aortic injury, and what are the source-stated risks?
column16:
Open surgical repair is used only when anatomy precludes TEVAR.
Source-stated risks: increased cord ischemia and death; 14% develop paraplegia related to cross-clamp time, and overall mortality is 20%.
Exam pivot: open repair is the fallback pathway, not the preferred pathway when endovascular anatomy is suitable.

column15: What is the role of transesophageal echocardiography in traumatic aortic injury evaluation?
column16:
TEE can demonstrate intimal tear, transection, and hemopericardium.
It may be technically difficult in severely injured patients and is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
Exam pivot: TEE is a problem-specific adjunct, not the default diagnostic test when CTA is feasible.

column15: What is the role of angiography in traumatic aortic injury evaluation?
column16:
Angiography was the previous gold standard but is now used for endovascular repair.
It carries a small risk of rupture.
Source-stated radiograph-driven inefficiency: using chest radiography as the guide, 10 negative angiograms were performed for each TAI diagnosed.
Exam pivot: CTA replaced diagnostic angiography for diagnosis/planning; angiography lives mainly in the treatment workflow.
column21:
🩻 Traumatic Aortic Injury
Traumatic aortic injury is an aortic wall disruption, usually from high-velocity blunt deceleration trauma, with CTA as the key diagnostic and treatment-planning test. The dominant board move is to connect indirect radiographic mediastinal hemorrhage signs to direct CTA signs, then separate true injury from isthmus mimics.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer synthesis; Core coverage from Core Radiology, 2nd ed., Vascular Imaging, Aortic trauma, Vascular:547-548, plus IR:604 treatment note; RadPrimer article: Traumatic Aortic Injury.
🧾 Abbreviations / Synonyms
  • TAI = traumatic aortic injury; related terms include ATAI, BTAI, BAT, BAI, BTAR, aortic transection, and traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm.
🧭 Overview / Mechanism
  • Aortic wall disruption ranges from intimomedial injury to transection; blunt rapid deceleration is most common, while penetrating trauma is less common.
  • Core emphasizes relatively fixed injury levels: aortic root, isthmus, and hiatus; the aortic isthmus is the most common site.
  • RadPrimer localizes the isthmus injury to the distal aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum and states the aortic isthmus accounts for about 90% of cases.
  • Mechanisms include rapid differential deceleration/shear, torsion/stretching, water-hammer intraaortic pressure, viscous response, and osseous pinch.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • Radiography / initial trauma clue
    • Wide mediastinum and aortic contour abnormality are the best initial radiographic clues.
    • Normal chest radiograph is exceedingly rare; RadPrimer gives a negative predictive value of 98%.
    • Indirect signs include mediastinal widening, AP window obscuration, tracheal/tube deviation, left apical cap, widened paravertebral/paratracheal stripe, and inferior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus.
    • A first rib fracture indicates severe trauma and possible TAI.
  • CTA
    • CTA is the imaging modality of choice / Core gold standard for suspected traumatic aortic injury.
    • Direct signs: intimomedial flap or intimal irregularity, pseudoaneurysm/contained rupture, irregular aortic contour, abrupt caliber change, dissection flap, intramural hematoma, and rarely active extravasation.
    • Indirect sign: periaortic or mediastinal hemorrhage; hemorrhage contacting the aortic wall is concerning, while hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by fat plane may be venous.
    • Morphologically normal aorta with no mediastinal hematoma has 100% negative predictive value for excluding TAI.
  • Other modalities
    • MR generally has no role in acute trauma; it may be used to identify or follow intramural hematoma in stable patients.
    • TEE can demonstrate intimal tear, transection, and hemopericardium; it is most commonly used intraoperatively when CT cannot be performed.
    • Angiography was the prior gold standard and is now used for endovascular repair.
🎯 Classification / Management Pivots
  • Society of Vascular Surgery grading: Grade I intimal injury/minimal aortic injury; Grade II intramural hematoma; Grade III pseudoaneurysm; Grade IV rupture.
  • Minimal aortic injury: subcentimeter intimomedial abnormality without external contour deformity; Core notes intimal-only lesions with small flap less than 1 cm and no/little periaortic hematoma.
  • Grade I is the only injury routinely managed nonoperatively with close monitoring and CTA follow-up; RadPrimer notes growing support for selected grade II/III conservative management.
  • Anti-impulse / blood pressure control is paramount and reduces rupture risk from 12% to 1.5%; RadPrimer goal: SBP ≤100 mm Hg, MAP ≤80 mm Hg, HR ≤100 bpm.
  • TEVAR is recommended over open repair for grade II-IV injuries when anatomy is suitable; open repair is reserved for anatomy precluding TEVAR.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis / Mimics
  • Wide mediastinum false positives include rotation, supine positioning, expiratory imaging, mediastinal fat, and tortuous great vessels.
  • Ductus diverticulum is an anteromedial isthmus outpouching with smooth, gently sloping shoulders and no intimomedial flap.
  • Other mimics include fusiform proximal descending aortic enlargement, aortic spindle, atherosclerotic ulceration, bronchial-intercostal trunk infundibulum, artifacts, plaque, and adjacent opacified vessels.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Contained rupture or pseudoaneurysm should not be reported in a way that implies stability.
  • Chronic pseudoaneurysm occurs in about 2% of survivors and may appear as a peripherally calcified mass at the aortopulmonary window with thrombus.
  • Thin-section CTA with multiplanar reformations is essential for differentiating true injury from mimics; high-pitch or cardiac-gated CT may help with equivocal pulsation or motion artifacts.
⭐ Super Summary
  • In blunt high-energy trauma, do not stop at a wide mediastinum: CTA looks for a direct aortic wall sign, especially at the isthmus.
  • The safest discriminator is wall injury plus adjacent periaortic hematoma; smooth isthmus outpouching without flap or hemorrhage favors ductus diverticulum.
  • Management hinges on grade and stability: minimal intimal injuries can be observed; pseudoaneurysm/rupture-level injuries move toward TEVAR and anti-impulse control.
